Place this in the third box of the Knowledge Table. st What I want to Know What I have Learnt ee Activity 2 What is the best way to solve a dispute? Read the story of ‘The Monkey and the Scale’. Then answer the questions. The Monkey and the Scale Once upon a time, there were two cats named Fulla and Yasmina. Fulla and Yasmina were good friends and would often help each other look for food. One morning, the cats woke up and looked out the window, only to see everything covered in snow. They felt cold and sad, knowing that finding food in this weather would be very difficult. The cats waited until they were so hungry they had no choice but to go in search of food. They had been searching for a long time, when suddenly Fulla saw a sardine in front of them. Yasmina picked it up in her mouth and the two friends hurried back home. Once they were home, Yasmina said, ‘I will eat the whole sardine myself, as I was the one who carried it back home and I’m very hungry’. ‘I should eat the sardine because I found it,’ objected Fulla, ‘and I am hungry, too!’ Soon they were screeching and hissing and snarling at each other; they were fighting so loudly that a monkey that was passing by stopped to see what was causing all that noise. Unable to find a solution, the cats decided to tell their story to the monkey and see what he thought. The monkey thought hard about their problem. He sat for a long while quietly and then he said, ‘You should go and see the judge. He holds court under the giant fig tree.’ Then, the monkey hurried to the tree. He quickly put on a judge’s robe and sat behind the table waiting for the cats I to arrive. Soon the cats approached the judge, each of them looking angrily at the other. Fulla spoke first. ‘Your Honour, as we were out in the cold looking for food, I found a sardine. Clearly the sard

units.unitTitle: Respect and Tolerance in a Diverse Community

lessons.lessonTitle: Tolerance and Its Relation to Diversity
objectives: In this lesson, students will be supported in
understanding, recognising and valuing the cultural
diversity which exists in their classroom.
lessons.content: Moral, Social & Cultural Studies Explain to the students that you are going to play a new game called ‘Find someone who...’ This activity is designed to help them learn more about their friends and the different cultures in their class. Before the lesson, prepare a worksheet like the one shown below. In this lesson, students will be supported in understanding, recognising and valuing the cultural diversity which exists in their classroom. ‘Find Someone Who...’ worksheet Child-friendly version of the United Nations Declaration of Human Rights Cultural symbols (student-led but teacher brings one in advance to model the activity) Give reasons why it is important to respect the beliefs, values and traditions of others. Articulate some of their own prejudices and provide strategies to overcome these prejudices. • Give reasons why it is important to respect the beliefs, values and traditions of others. • Articulate some of their own prejudices and provide strategies to overcome these prejudices. activities: Activity 1 (15 min): Explain to the students that you are going to play a new game called ‘Find someone who...’ This activity is designed to help them learn more about their friends and the different cultures in their class. Before the lesson, prepare a worksheet like the one shown below. Find someone who…. Lesson Objectives In this lesson, students will be supported in understanding, recognising and valuing the

Activity 2 (15 min): Ask students to read the text about respecting other cultures. Emphasise that people should learn about other cultures either through facts or through their own experience. After they do so, people should respect and tolerate different cultures. And this should be mutual. Explain that prejudice comes from things we believe about other cultures that are not based on fact or actual experienc

Activity 3 (10 min): Put students into small groups (of about five students) and allow them time to read through the text about the UN Declaration of Human Rights. Remind students that, while there are many rights, they will be focusing on the ones listed here, mainly Article 27, which provides people with the right to participate in the cultural life of their community. Suggested Answers a. After World War II

Activity 4 (10 min): Engage in a brief discussion with the students to draw out how they better understand culture as a result of the activities. Suggested Answers a. Explain to the students that sometimes is it useful to think of culture like an iceberg. Just like an iceberg, some of culture is very visible and easy to see as it is exterior and above the surface (for example, clothes, language, festivals, food

Activity 5 (10 min): Organise students in a standing circle, each holding a picture they have drawn of a symbol or artefact of their culture. In the circle, ask each student to share with their classmates what the item is, why they chose it and what type of cultural significance it holds for them. The following questions can act as stimuli to guide the discussion: • What made you choose to draw this item over

lessons.lessonTitle: Exploring Our Own Beliefs and Attitudes
objectives: In this lesson, students will learn to recognise the
similarities as well as the differences that exist
between people of all backgrounds. They will also
learn about the importance of having empathy for
people who have different values and traditions.
lessons.content: Moral, Social & Cultural Studies In this lesson, students will learn to recognise the similarities as well as the differences that exist between people of all backgrounds. They will also learn about the importance of having empathy for people who have different values and traditions. Photograph of each student Give reasons why it is important to respect the beliefs, values and traditions of others. Articulate some of your own prejudices and offer strategies to overcome them. Demonstrate respect for differences and treat peers as equals in the classroom and • Behave virtuously, for example by demonstrating a capacity for patience or forgiveness, in dealings with other people. When their drawings are complete, students should compare them with their classmates. Tell students that all of the drawings are very good even t

Activity 2 (15 min): Read the article about a student who had a learning disabliity called dyslexia. Explain that dyslexia is quite common in schools today but that years ago, before it was recognised as a learning disability, people who had this disorder were considered slow and less able. They were often made fun of because their fellow classmates did not understand their differences. Suggested Answers a. T

Activity 3 (10 min): Read the article about the need for diversity in today’s world. Emphasise that diversity in the workplace is necessary because different skills are needed to run businesses. You can give the school as an example: there is a need for different roles such as managers, accountants, teachers, vendors, students, cleaners, bus drivers, etc. Explain to students what innovation means: the applicatio

Activity 5 (5 min): Ask each student to think about themselves as individuals. In what way are they proud to be different from everyone else in the group? Encourage them to think about the following: • Appearance, talent, skill, trait or personality • Ways in which the things they like or dislike may be different from other people in the group Invite them to write down two sentences on a sticky label and place

Activity 4 (15 min): Ask students to read the text about Dubai and then answer the questions. Suggested Answers a. Living in a diverse community requires tolerance and respect. It also creates an incentive to learn from other cultures and perhaps adopt some of their habits. New cultures enriched Dubai by introducing different customs and ways of doing thing; for example, food, clothes, new ideas in work. As for t

objectives: Understand how preparing museum exhibits can help
develop our cultural competence.
lessons.content: Moral, Social & Cultural Studies Understand how preparing museum exhibits can help develop our cultural competence. Colours, paint, markers, etc Explain and give examples of what is meant by terms such as ‘multiculturalism’, ‘diversity’, ‘cultural heritage’ and ‘inclusivity’. Research and analyse how and why Emirati culture has changed over time and the ways in which cultural institutions maintain the cultural Discuss with their peers the concept of what should be valued and maintained, and what can be learnt from our past to inform the future. Demonstrate their ‘cultural competence’ by participating in a school or ‘community based’ How the UAE Grew into the Diverse, Inclusive Society • Explain and give examples of what is meant by terms such as ‘multiculturalism’, ‘diversity’, ‘cultural heritage’ and ‘inclusivity’. • Research and analyse how and why Emirati culture has changed over time and the ways in which cultural institutions maintain the cultural heritage. • Discuss with their peers the concept of what should be valued and maintained, and what can be learnt from our past to inform the future. • Demonstrate their ‘cultural competence’ by participating in a school or community-based activity All rights reserved. activities: Activity 1 (10 min): The teacher should find a place in the school to display the exhibit. Maybe a common hall or an empty classroom. First, ask students to share their research from their homework assignment from the previous week. Address any questions that students raise. Next, explain that now that the students know what objects they want to display in their exhibit, they need to decide how they will displa

Activity 2 (15 min): Ask students to read the story about Jamila and her family museum exhibit. As they read about the exhibit, ask students to think about their class exhibit and what they can learn from Jamila’s experience. Discuss the questions in the student book. Suggested Answers For Beginners • Jamila wanted to tell her uncle the story of her family. She wanted to do this in an unusual way, similar to a m

Activity 3 (15 min): Congratulate the students on selecting different objects for their exhibit. Each student knows why their objects are important to the theme of the exhibit. However, the exhibit visitors also need to know why these objects are important. What’s the story behind each object? This is where museum labels come in! Ask students to read the short article about museum labelling. Address any question

Activity 4 (15 min): Explain that putting together a museum exhibit involves many tasks. The class will need to work together as a team to create the exhibit. The most important thing here is teamwork. In museums people work in teams in order to get the exhibitions ready. That way each person works to their own talents. Tell the students that you will be dividing them into groups according to different tasks:

Activity 5 (5 min): Take some time to congratulate the students on what they have achieved. Give them positive feedback and encouragement. Next, ask students to think about what they hope to achieve from the exhibit. What was the purpose of the exhibit? Did they achieve this? Ask them to write their reflections in the student book. Ask them to share their thoughts with the class, if they feel comfortable doing

Explain to students that they are about to meet two new visitors to their class. Invite them to think of questions which might help them find out more about the visitors and their culture. Explain that the visitors can only answer ‘yes’ or ‘no’ so they should formulate their questions accordingly. Ask for two volunteers from the class who will play the roles of the visitors. While the rest of the class are thinking of questions, take the two volunteers aside. Explain that there is a trick in this game: no matter what the question is, they should answer ‘yes’ to anyone who is smiling. Are you from another country? Do you speak another language?

Activity 2 (15 min): Read the article about a student who had a learning disabliity called dyslexia. They were often made fun of because their fellow classmates did not understand their differences. Suggested Answers a. T

This could be because of gender, race, cultural background, or something as simple as being shy or having freckles! Ask students how they would act if they were a witness to bullying or Explain that being a bystander means that they would take no action whatsoever. Being a bystander entails watching the behaviour and the discomfort of the target and simply doing nothing. This shows apathy, which is the opposite of empathy. It means that the witness is unmoved by the bullying and feels no sympathy for the target nor anger at the perpetrators. something. A pre-conceived basis in knowledge or fact. activities: Activity 1 (5 min): Ask students if they have ever come across behaviour like that described in the questions. Students should recognise that these are all instances of bullying. Suggested Answers The answer to the question ‘Why?’ is often some form of prejudice or discrimination against someone who is different in some way. This could be because of gender, race, cultural background, or something as simple as be

Activity 2 (15 min): Read the story about prejudice and explain that it comes from a fear of the unknown. In history, when people lived in smaller communities, and resources were scarce, it was common for neighbouring communities to compete for the same resources. This led to a deep distrust of anyone who did not belong to the same group as them. In modern society, however, nations are not as homogenous as the

Activity 3 (10 min): Read the story about a boy who had been influenced by stories he had heard about people from another country. Explain to students that prejudice is based on unreasonable likes and dislikes of a person or a group of people. The prejudice is unreasonable because it does not have any basis in fact. Suggested Answers a. He had this view because he had listened to his uncle without taking time

Activity 4 (15 min): Explain to students that they will participate in an activity called a ‘walking debate’. Clear space on the floor and put posters titled ‘Yes’, ‘No’ and ‘Maybe’ on the left-hand side, middle and right-hand side of a classroom wall. Read out the following statements and give the students time to think about their answers before taking their position beside the relevant poster. Walking Debat

Activity 5 (10 min): This activity is a project for students to research at home and present in the next lesson. Ask each student to list five cultures. Top of the list will be the one they like most and the last will be the one they like least. Ask students to be honest as this is very important for their project.
